candidate_sources.title: Bacterial blight on soybean | UMN Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Bacterial blight of soybean caused by Pseudomonas savastanoi pv. glycinea. Symptoms: small angular lesions, water-soaked at the center surrounded by a yellow-green halo. Later spots coalesce to form dark-brown necrotic areas with yellow margins. Disease favored by cool (70-80F), wet weather. Management: plant disease-tolerant cultivars, use pathogen-free seed, appropriate rotations, avoid field cultivation when foliage is wet.

candidate_sources.title: Bacterial Blight of Soybean | NC State Extension Publications
candidate_sources.snippet: Bacterial Blight of Soybean caused by Pseudomonas syringae pv. glycinea. Light to dark brown necrotic regions that are vein-limited giving an angular appearance on young leaves, often with chlorosis surrounding the necrotic region. Symptoms usually begin in the upper canopy. A yellowish-green halo appears around the edge of water-soaked tissue. Development promoted by cool, wet weather (70-80F).

candidate_sources.title: Bacterial Blight Extended Version | OSU Soybean Diseases
candidate_sources.snippet: OSU extension page on bacterial blight of soybean caused by Pseudomonas savastanoi pv. glycinea. Detailed symptoms, pathogen biology, disease cycle, and management recommendations including resistant cultivar selection and crop rotation.

candidate_sources.title: Pseudomonas savastanoi pv. glycinea (bacterial blight of soybean) | CABI Compendium
candidate_sources.snippet: CABI Compendium datasheet for Pseudomonas savastanoi pv. glycinea, the bacterial blight pathogen of soybean. Includes taxonomy, distribution, symptoms, biology, host range, and management.

candidate_sources.title: Bacterial Diseases of Soybean - University of Nebraska-Lincoln Extension (G2058)
candidate_sources.snippet: UNL Extension publication covering bacterial diseases of soybean including bacterial blight (Pseudomonas syringae pv. glycinea), bacterial pustule, and wildfire. Discusses symptoms, disease cycle, and management strategies.

candidate_sources.title: Bacterial pustule on soybean | UMN Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Symptoms begin as small, light green spots (not water-soaked) with raised centers on the upper and lower surfaces of leaves, with light-colored pustules (blisters) developing in the center of lesions. Caused by Xanthomonas axonopodis pv. glycines. Plant resistant varieties and use pathogen-free seed; rotate soybeans with non-host crops.

candidate_sources.title: Bacterial Pustule | CropWatch - University of Nebraska–Lincoln
candidate_sources.snippet: Bacterial pustule develops in warm (85-90°F), moist conditions. Leaf wetness is required for infection. Differentiated by lack of water soaking at initial site of infection, less chlorosis around lesions, and formation of tiny tan bumps on undersides of leaves. Caused by Xanthomonas axonopodis pv. glycines.

candidate_sources.title: Bacterial Pustule - Soybean Disease - Soybean Research & Information Network (SRIN)
candidate_sources.snippet: Initial infection results in development of tiny pale green spots on new leaves with raised centers; small brown-colored pustules form in the middle of the spots and the spots turn yellow. Spots may merge, forming large irregularly yellowing lesions. Cultural practices: plant resistant varieties, rotate with non-host crops, incorporate crop residue.

candidate_sources.title: Bacterial pustule - Cornell CALS
candidate_sources.snippet: Bacterial pustule of soybean caused by Xanthomonas axonopodis pv. glycines. Cornell College of Agriculture and Life Sciences page describing symptoms, conditions, and management.

candidate_sources.title: Xanthomonas axonopodis pv. glycines (soyabean bacterial pustule) - CABI Plantwise Plus Knowledge Bank
candidate_sources.snippet: CABI datasheet on Xanthomonas axonopodis pv. glycines, the causal agent of soyabean bacterial pustule, covering distribution, symptoms, biology, and management.

candidate_sources.title: Bean pod mottle virus on soybean | UMN Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Bean pod mottle virus causes green to yellow mottling of young leaves, and in severe infections, leaves may become distorted. BPMV is transmitted by leaf-feeding beetles, specifically the bean leaf beetle (Cerotoma trifurcata).

candidate_sources.title: Bean Pod Mottle Virus | NC State Extension Publications
candidate_sources.snippet: Symptoms associated with BPMV include mild to severe chlorotic mottling or mosaic and rugosity (distortion or wrinkling) on foliage and pods, stunting, and delayed maturity. BPMV is a member of the genus Comovirus in the family Comoviridae, comprised of a bipartite, positive, single-stranded RNA.

candidate_sources.title: Bean Pod Mottle of Soybean | Crop Protection Network
candidate_sources.snippet: Bean pod mottle virus (BPMV) can cause yield losses as high as 52% and reduce seed quality. Symptoms first appear on younger leaves and can range from mild chlorotic mottling of foliage to a severe mosaic. Green stem may also occur. Seed from infected plants may appear smoky in color or have reduced size and quality due to seed coat mottling.

candidate_sources.title: Bean Pod Mottle Virus | CropWatch | Nebraska
candidate_sources.snippet: Bean pod mottle virus is transmitted by the bean leaf beetle. Management strategies include insecticide seed treatments and foliar sprays targeting bean leaf beetle, planting certified virus-free seed, and managing weed hosts.

candidate_sources.title: Cercospora Leaf Blight of Soybean | NC State Extension Publications
candidate_sources.snippet: First symptom is the development of light purple spots on the top surface of leaves; areas expand and deepen to reddish-purple or bronze; leaves appear leathery and 'sunburned'. Caused by Cercospora kikuchii (and C. flagellaris). Favored by hot (68-86 F) and humid (>92.5%) conditions with prolonged dew periods.

candidate_sources.title: Cercospora leaf blight and purple seed stain on soybean | UMN Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Cercospora leaf blight and purple seed stain on soybean caused by Cercospora kikuchii. Leaf symptoms begin as light purple color with leathery appearance; sun-exposed upper leaves develop reddish-purple to bronze discoloration. Infected seeds show pink-to-purple discoloration on seed coats.

candidate_sources.title: An Overview of Cercospora Leaf Blight of Soybean | Crop Protection Network
candidate_sources.snippet: Overview publication covering symptoms, pathogen biology (Cercospora kikuchii, C. flagellaris), disease cycle, environmental conditions favoring development, and integrated management of Cercospora leaf blight in soybean.

candidate_sources.title: Cercospora Leaf Blight and Purple Seed Stain in Soybean | University of Delaware Cooperative Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: University of Delaware factsheet on Cercospora leaf blight and purple seed stain caused by Cercospora kikuchii in soybean, including symptoms on leaves and seed, disease cycle, conditions favoring infection, and management recommendations.

candidate_sources.title: Soybean Charcoal Rot | NC State Extension Publications
candidate_sources.snippet: Mid to late season aboveground symptoms include light brown to grey superficial lesions in the stem. A characteristic sign of the pathogen on soybean is the presence of microsclerotia in the vascular tissue, in the pith, and mature and dry pods. Caused by Macrophomina phaseolina.

candidate_sources.title: Charcoal Rot in Soybean | Cooperative Extension | University of Delaware
candidate_sources.snippet: University of Delaware extension factsheet on charcoal rot in soybean caused by Macrophomina phaseolina, covering symptoms, pathogen biology, and management practices.

candidate_sources.title: Charcoal rot on soybean | UMN Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Plants can be infected any time during the growing season, and many plants are infected early. Symptoms appear after plants enter reproductive stages or become stressed. Hot, dry conditions favor symptom development.

candidate_sources.title: An Overview of Charcoal Rot of Soybean
candidate_sources.snippet: Crop Protection Network publication providing an overview of charcoal rot of soybean caused by Macrophomina phaseolina, including symptoms, disease cycle, and management.

candidate_sources.title: Extended Version | OSU Soybean Diseases - Charcoal Rot
candidate_sources.snippet: Ohio State University extended version factsheet on soybean charcoal rot. Covers identification, pathogen Macrophomina phaseolina, microsclerotia signs, and stem disease symptoms.

candidate_sources.title: Advancing Our Understanding of Charcoal Rot in Soybeans | Journal of Integrated Pest Management
candidate_sources.snippet: Peer-reviewed article in Journal of Integrated Pest Management advancing understanding of charcoal rot in soybeans caused by Macrophomina phaseolina.

candidate_sources.title: Downy mildew on soybean | UMN Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Initial symptoms include small, light green spots on upper leaf surfaces that enlarge and turn pale to bright yellow, and may coalesce into large irregular brown areas. Tan to gray tufts of fungal growth often develop on lower leaf surfaces. Caused by Peronospora manshurica.

candidate_sources.title: Downy Mildew of Soybean
candidate_sources.snippet: Downy mildew is caused by the fungal-like organism Peronospora manshurica. Symptoms include light green spots on upper leaf surfaces that turn pale to bright yellow with tan to gray tufts of fungal growth on lower leaf surfaces under wet and humid conditions.

candidate_sources.title: Downy Mildew - Soybean Disease - Soybean Research & Information Network - SRIN
candidate_sources.snippet: Downy mildew of soybean is caused by Peronospora manshurica. Dew and high humidity favor disease development. Management includes crop rotation, resistant varieties, and burying infested residue.

candidate_sources.title: Downy mildew - Soybeans - Cornell CALS
candidate_sources.snippet: Downy mildew on soybean caused by Peronospora manshurica. Symptoms appear as light green to yellow spots on upper leaf surfaces with tufts of fungal growth on lower surfaces during humid conditions.

candidate_sources.title: Frogeye Leaf Spot of Soybean | NC State Extension Publications
candidate_sources.snippet: Frogeye leaf spot (FLS), caused by Cercospora sojina, produces small dark water-soaked lesions that enlarge to gray-brown centers with reddish-purple margins on soybean leaves; management includes resistant varieties, crop rotation, and fungicides.

candidate_sources.title: Frogeye Leaf Spot of Soybean - Crop Protection Network
candidate_sources.snippet: Detailed encyclopedia entry on frogeye leaf spot of soybean caused by Cercospora sojina, with information on symptoms on leaves, stems, pods and seeds; environmental conditions favoring disease (77-86F, >90% RH); and integrated management.

candidate_sources.title: Purdue Extension BP-131-W Frogeye Leaf Spot
candidate_sources.snippet: Purdue Extension factsheet on Frogeye Leaf Spot of soybean caused by Cercospora sojina, covering disease symptoms, signs, life cycle, and management with resistant varieties, crop rotation, tillage, and fungicide applications.

candidate_sources.title: Frogeye leaf spot on soybean | UMN Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: University of Minnesota Extension page on frogeye leaf spot caused by Cercospora sojina; describes circular lesions with gray centers and reddish-purple borders, conditions favoring infection, and management including QoI fungicide resistance concerns.

candidate_sources.title: Phytophthora root and stem rot on soybean | UMN Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Phytophthora sojae is a fungal-like pathogen (oomycete) that survives in soil as oospores for 5-10 years. Infected stems appear bruised and soft, secondary roots are rotted, leaves turn yellow and brown, and plants can wilt and die. Disease favored by wet weather, poorly drained areas, clay/compacted soils, and soil temperatures above 60°F.

candidate_sources.title: Phytopthora Root and Stem Rot of Soybean | NC State Extension Publications
candidate_sources.snippet: Detailed factsheet on Phytophthora root and stem rot of soybean caused by Phytophthora sojae. Symptoms include brown to purple lesion on stem below soil line progressing upward a few nodes, yellowing of lower leaves between veins and margins, general yellowing of upper leaves, stunting, and root rot.

candidate_sources.title: Phytophthora Root and Stem Rot of Soybean | Crop Protection Network
candidate_sources.snippet: Comprehensive encyclopedia entry on Phytophthora root and stem rot of soybean. Covers symptoms (dark chocolate brown discoloration extending from base of plant up the stem), pathogen biology, environmental conditions favoring disease, and management strategies including Rps resistance genes (Rps1a, Rps1c, Rps1k, Rps3a, Rps6) and seed treatments with mefenoxam, metalaxyl, ethaboxam, or oxathiapiprolin.

candidate_sources.title: Phytophthora Damping-off and Root Rot of Soybean | Ohioline (Ohio State University)
candidate_sources.snippet: Ohio State University extension factsheet covering Phytophthora damping-off and root rot of soybean caused by Phytophthora sojae. Discusses seedling damping-off, mid-season root and stem rot symptoms, and integrated management.

candidate_sources.title: Sclerotinia Stem Rot (White Mold) of Soybean | Ohioline
candidate_sources.snippet: Sclerotinia stem rot of soybean is caused by the fungus Sclerotinia sclerotiorum. Diseased plants first appear wilted with grayish green leaves that later turn brown to tan. Stems have grayish to bleached white lesions covered by white fluffy mycelia under high moisture. Sclerotia can be observed inside or outside of stems and pods.

candidate_sources.title: Sclerotinia stem rot (white mold) on soybean | UMN Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: Lesions usually develop first at stem nodes during or after flowering. Tops of plants become grayish-green and then wilt and die. Infected stems become soft and watery, and become covered with white moldy growth in moist conditions. Signs include white cottony mycelia and sclerotia on and inside infected plant stems and pods.

candidate_sources.title: White Mold of Soybean | Crop Protection Network
candidate_sources.snippet: White mold (Sclerotinia stem rot) of soybean is caused by Sclerotinia sclerotiorum. Symptoms include water-soaked stem lesions that progress above and below infected nodes and eventually encircle the stem. Infected stems become bleached and stringy. Management includes host resistance, crop rotation, tillage, canopy management, and fungicide application during flowering (R1-R3).

candidate_sources.title: White Mold - Purdue Extension (BP-43-W)
candidate_sources.snippet: Purdue Extension publication on white mold (Sclerotinia stem rot) of soybean caused by Sclerotinia sclerotiorum, covering symptoms, disease cycle, and management.

candidate_sources.title: Southern Blight of Soybean | NC State Extension Publications
candidate_sources.snippet: Southern blight of soybean is caused by the fungus Sclerotium rolfsii. Symptoms vary depending on growth stage; seedling infection results in pre- or post-emergence damping-off. Later in the season, entire plants may become yellow and wilt with a dark brown lesion girdling the stem at the soil surface and white moldy growth.

candidate_sources.title: Southern Blight of Soybeans - Crop Protection Network
candidate_sources.snippet: Southern blight is caused by Agroathelia rolfsii (formerly Sclerotium rolfsii / Athelia rolfsii), a soilborne fungus with a wide host range of more than 200 plant species. Once considered a minor issue in soybean in the South, it has become more problematic, especially in fields with a history of the disease.

candidate_sources.title: Southern blight, southern stem blight, white mold - APS
candidate_sources.snippet: APS lesson on southern blight (also called southern stem blight, white mold) caused by Sclerotium rolfsii. Discusses symptoms, sclerotia formation, host range, and management of this soilborne pathogen on soybean and other crops.

candidate_sources.title: Southern Blight - University of Kentucky Plant Pathology Fact Sheet (PPFS-GEN-16)
candidate_sources.snippet: Southern blight (Figure 1) affects hundreds of host plants including soybean. Caused by Sclerotium rolfsii, the pathogen survives as sclerotia in soil for 3-4 years. White fungal mats and tan to reddish-brown sclerotia form at the stem base.

candidate_sources.title: Asian Soybean Rust | NC State Extension Publications
candidate_sources.snippet: Asian soybean rust, caused by the fungus Phakopsora pachyrhizi, is a soybean pathogen described for the first time in the Southern portion of North Carolina in 2005. Two species of rust fungi cause soybean rust - Phakopsora pachyrhizi (aggressive Asian pathogen) and P. meibomiae (mild pathogen). Lesions tend to be angular with 2-5 mm diameter, and can affect not only leaves, but may be observed on petioles, pods, and stems.

candidate_sources.title: Soybean rust | UMN Extension
candidate_sources.snippet: University of Minnesota Extension factsheet on soybean rust caused by Phakopsora pachyrhizi. Includes identification, scouting recommendations, environmental conditions favoring disease, and management strategies. Scout the lower canopy of fields weekly, especially during wet weather after soybeans begin to flower.

candidate_sources.title: Soybean Rust | Ohioline - The Ohio State University
candidate_sources.snippet: Ohio State University Extension factsheet on Soybean Rust. A common symptom is the formation of gray-green, tan to dark-brown, or reddish-brown lesions that may become erumpant under the lesions on leaflets. Uredia develop more frequently in lesions on the lower surface of the leaf than on the upper surface. Lesions are associated with leaf chlorosis; high lesion density can cause defoliation and early maturity of the plant.

candidate_sources.title: Asian Soybean Rust | CropWatch | Nebraska
candidate_sources.snippet: University of Nebraska CropWatch resource on Asian Soybean Rust caused by Phakopsora pachyrhizi. Provides information on symptoms, pathogen biology, scouting, and management for soybean producers.
